question_title: Will Uganda report a second laboratory-confirmed case of Ebola (Bundibugyo virus) by May 31, 2026?
summary: The WHO DON602 report (published May 16, 2026) — one of the primary resolution sources — explicitly reports 2 laboratory-confirmed Ebola Bundibugyo cases in Uganda: the original imported case (elderly man who died May 14) and a second imported case confirmed May 16 in Kampala, epidemiologically unlinked to the first. This satisfies the resolution criteria, which require 2+ confirmed cases reported by WHO or credible sources during May 17-31. The fine print explicitly states a cumulative count of 2+ resolves YES. Multiple sources corroborate: Wikipedia, news outlets, and the WHO PHEIC declaration. I estimate 95% rather than higher because the question description appears to contradict the WHO report (stating 'no further confirmed cases'), suggesting the author may have been unaware of the second case when publishing the question on May 19, creating a small risk of disputed resolution. However, Metaculus typically resolves on stated criteria, and the evidence from the primary resolution source is unambiguous.

factors.description: WHO DON602 (primary resolution source) explicitly reports 2 confirmed Bundibugyo cases in Uganda as of May 16, 2026. The second case was confirmed on May 16 in Kampala — an individual returning from DRC, unlinked to the first case. Fine print states: 'If the reporting source provides a single cumulative confirmed-case count for Uganda, a value of 2 or more resolves YES.'

Multiple independent sources corroborate: Wikipedia article on '2026 Ituri Province Ebola epidemic', research agent synthesis from Reuters/CNN/BBC, and WHO PHEIC declaration all reference imported cases (plural) in Uganda's Kampala.

Resolution criteria explicitly specify window starting May 17 — as of that date, the WHO DON602 report showing 2 confirmed Uganda cases is already publicly available and subsequently referenced by CDC HAN on May 19.

Question description contradicts WHO DON602 by stating 'no further laboratory-confirmed Ebola cases have been reported in Uganda' — suggests question author may have been unaware of the second case, creating small risk of non-standard resolution interpretation.
CDC HAN from May 19 mentions only one patient in Uganda description, though this appears to be simplification rather than denial of the second case.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.95 (95% YES)**

## Key Reasoning

**The question has essentially already been answered.** The WHO DON602 report — published May 16, 2026, and listed as a primary resolution source — explicitly documents **2 laboratory-confirmed Ebola Bundibugyo cases in Uganda**:

1. **First case**: 59-year-old man from DRC, admitted to Kampala hospital May 11, died May 14, confirmed May 15
2. **Second case**: Confirmed May 16 in Kampala, individual returning from DRC, **unlinked** to the first case

The resolution criteria require "two or more laboratory-confirmed cases" reported by WHO or credible sources during May 17-31. The fine print states: *"If the reporting source provides a single cumulative confirmed-case count for Uganda, a value of 2 or more resolves YES."* This is clearly satisfied.

**Why not higher than 95%?** The question description itself states "no further laboratory-confirmed Ebola cases have been reported in Uganda" — contradicting its own primary resolution source. This suggests the question author may have been unaware of the second case when publishing on May 19, creating a small risk of non-standard resolution. However, Metaculus typically resolves on stated criteria, not author intent.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Uganda report a second laboratory-confirmed case of Ebola (Bundibugyo virus) by May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43542)
[Ebola disease caused by Bundibugyo virus, Democratic Republic of the Congo & Uganda](https://www.who.int/emergencies/disease-outbreak-news/item/2026-DON602)
[Ebola Disease Outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda](https://www.cdc.gov/han/php/notices/han00530.html)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, at any time between May 17, 2026 and May 31, 2026 (inclusive), the Uganda Ministry of Health, WHO, Africa CDC, or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) report that Uganda has identified two or more laboratory-confirmed cases of Ebola virus disease (Bundibugyo species) associated with the 2026 Ituri outbreak — i.e., at least one additional confirmed case beyond the single imported case (59-year-old man) reported on 14 May 2026. The additional case may be either imported or locally transmitted, and may be a fatal or surviving patient, so long as it is laboratory-confirmed (e.g., by PCR at the Uganda Virus Research Institute or another accredited reference laboratory) and reported as a confirmed Ebola case in Uganda. Otherwise the question resolves NO. The primary resolution source will be WHO AFRO outbreak updates (https://www.afro.who.int/health-topics/ebola-virus-disease) and the Africa CDC newsroom (https://africacdc.org/news/). If neither source has reported a second Ugandan confirmed case by 23:59 UTC on May 31, 2026, the question resolves NO.
fine_print: A "laboratory-confirmed case" means a person tested positive for Ebola virus (Bundibugyo species) by an accredited laboratory, as reported by an official public-health authority. Suspected, probable, or "pending confirmation" cases do not count. Cases reported in countries other than Uganda do not count toward this question. If the reporting source provides a single cumulative confirmed-case count for Uganda, a value of 2 or more resolves YES.

factors.description: WHO DON602 (primary resolution source) explicitly reports 2 confirmed Bundibugyo cases in Uganda as of May 16, 2026. The second case was confirmed on May 16 in Kampala — an individual returning from DRC, unlinked to the first case.
Wikipedia, research agent findings, and multiple major news outlets (Reuters, CNN, BBC) corroborate the second confirmed case in Uganda on May 16.
Resolution criteria specify window starts May 17 — as of that date, the WHO report showing 2 confirmed cases is already publicly available and referenced by subsequent reports (CDC HAN on May 19).

Question description appears to contradict the WHO DON602 by stating 'no further laboratory-confirmed Ebola cases have been reported in Uganda' — this creates some uncertainty about whether the question author was aware of the second case and whether resolution might be disputed.
CDC HAN from May 19 mentions only one patient in its Uganda description, though this may be simplification rather than contradiction of the second case.

input.anchor: Base rate for whether an already-reported event satisfying resolution criteria will be confirmed on resolution: ~90-95%. The event (second confirmed case) has already been reported by the primary resolution source.
input.assessment:
The question asks whether Uganda will report a second lab-confirmed Ebola Bundibugyo case by May 31, 2026. The WHO DON602 report, published May 16, 2026, explicitly states there are 2 confirmed cases in Uganda — the original imported case (elderly man, died May 14) and a second imported case confirmed May 16 in Kampala, unlinked to the first. This is confirmed by multiple credible sources including research agent findings citing Reuters, CNN, BBC, and Wikipedia.

The resolution criteria specify "at any time between May 17, 2026 and May 31, 2026 (inclusive)" — the WHO report with 2 confirmed cases was already available by May 17. The fine print states: "If the reporting source provides a single cumulative confirmed-case count for Uganda, a value of 2 or more resolves YES." The WHO DON602 satisfies this.

The main source of uncertainty is a discrepancy in the question description, which states "no further laboratory-confirmed Ebola cases have been reported in Uganda" — this appears to be based on information from before the second case was confirmed later on May 16. The question author may have been unaware of the second case when writing the description. However, resolution should follow the stated criteria, not the description's snapshot.

Additionally, the CDC HAN from May 19 mentions only one patient in its Uganda section, but this appears to be a simplification focused on the fatal imported case, not a denial of the second case. The WHO report (primary resolution source) is clear.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is ~92% based on the event already being reported by the primary resolution source. Factors push this higher given strong multi-source confirmation. The remaining ~5% uncertainty comes from: (1) possible retraction of the second case, (2) question author potentially interpreting criteria differently than the literal text, (3) small chance the WHO DON602 report is inaccurate or gets revised. The discrepancy in the question description is concerning but the resolution criteria are explicit and the WHO report is definitive.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether the question author was aware of the second case when writing the question — and if not, whether they might interpret the resolution criteria in a non-literal way. 2. Whether the second case confirmation is robust or could be retracted. 3. Whether the CDC HAN's mention of only one case reflects an actual disagreement about case counts.
input.tool_audit: Research agent: Very useful — found the second confirmed case from WHO DON602 and multiple corroborating sources. Web search: Confirmed findings with CDC HAN, Wikipedia, and news sources. Fetch URL on WHO DON602: Confirmed 2 confirmed Uganda cases. Fetch URL on CDC HAN: Provided detailed report but only mentioned one Uganda case (ambiguous). Metaculus coherence links: No related questions found. All tools functioned correctly.
input.update_triggers: 1. WHO or Uganda MoH retracting the second confirmed case. 2. Question author commenting on the question to clarify resolution intent. 3. Additional reports explicitly denying a second confirmed case in Uganda.
input.process_reflection: The research agent was highly effective for this question, quickly finding the critical piece of information (second confirmed case on May 16). The multi-source verification through web search and URL fetching was smooth. One minor friction point: the second research question about DRC outbreak details failed to retrieve live data, but this was compensated by the first research query's comprehensive results. The forecasting scaffolding worked well for this 'already happened' type question where the key task is verifying whether criteria are met rather than predicting future events.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Evidence Grounding — All factors verified

**Factor 1 (WHO DON602 reports 2 confirmed Uganda cases, logit=+3.5):** The trace shows a direct fetch_url of WHO DON602 returning: "Total confirmed cases in Uganda: 2" with details of both the first (elderly man, died May 14) and second (confirmed May 16, Kampala, unlinked) cases. ✅ Fully grounded.

**Factor 2 (Media corroboration, logit=+1.5):** The trace shows Wikipedia's article on the "2026 Ituri Province Ebola epidemic" and web search results mentioning the outbreak across CNN, BBC, Reuters, etc. The research agent's synthesis attributes second-case confirmation to these sources, though the direct snippets from the web_search tool focus on the outbreak broadly. The core claim (second case is corroborated beyond WHO alone) is grounded, though individual outlet confirmations come via the research agent's synthesis rather than direct fetches. Minor issue, doesn't change the conclusion.

**Factors 3-5 (timing, question contradiction, CDC HAN):** All grounded. The resolution criteria text explicitly states May 17 start date. The question background text says "no further laboratory-confirmed Ebola cases have been reported in Uganda." The CDC HAN text uses singular "a patient" for Uganda. All verifiable in the trace.

### Pre-Publication Event — Case 1 (Legitimate)

The second case was confirmed May 16, the resolution criteria specify a start date of May 17, and published_at is May 19. Since the resolution criteria specify an explicit start date (May 17) BEFORE published_at (May 19), this is Case 1. The criteria ask whether sources "report" 2+ cases during May 17-31 — the WHO DON602 published May 16 remains available throughout. The fine print explicitly states: "If the reporting source provides a single cumulative confirmed-case count for Uganda, a value of 2 or more resolves YES." This is satisfied. Not a problem.

### Double-Counting — Minor

Factors 1 and 2 both confirm the second case exists. Factor 2's media corroboration largely derives from WHO DON602 (Factor 1). The combined logit of +5.0 for these two factors is somewhat redundant, but corroboration from independent media does add marginal value (reduces retraction risk). Factor 3 (timing) is conceptually distinct from both. The slight double-counting doesn't materially change the forecast — even with Factor 2 removed, the probability would still be very high (~97% from Factor 1 alone, modestly offset by Factors 4-5).

### Resolution Misalignment — Minor

The resolution criteria cite "WHO AFRO outbreak updates" and "Africa CDC newsroom" as primary sources. The agent primarily used WHO DON602 (WHO HQ Disease Outbreak News). However, the broader resolution language also says "WHO" generically is acceptable, and DON602 is WHO's official report. The agent didn't specifically fetch WHO AFRO or Africa CDC pages, but the WHO DON602 is a more authoritative WHO-level source. This is a very minor gap that doesn't affect the forecast.

### Probability Assessment — Reasonable

95% appropriately accounts for the strong evidence (WHO DON602 clearly reports 2 cases, fine print explicitly covers cumulative counts) while leaving room for the genuine risk that question resolvers might interpret differently given the question description's contradiction ("no further laboratory-confirmed Ebola cases"). The counterargument about author intent is valid but Metaculus typically resolves on stated criteria, not inferred intent. My independent estimate would be 93-96%, fully consistent with the agent's 95%.

### Counterargument — Adequate

The agent constructs a coherent alternative story: question author may have intended a forward-looking question, CDC HAN only mentions one case, and the question wouldn't have been published if already resolved. This is a genuine, smart disagreer's argument. The agent also identifies specific "what would change my mind" scenarios (WHO correction, author comment, case reclassification). Confidence-in-estimate of 0.65 appropriately reflects the genuine ambiguity.

### Tool Audit — Clean

Agent reports 10 calls, 0 errors — matches ground truth metrics exactly. All tool descriptions are consistent with the metrics data.

### Conclusion

No hallucinated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment, resolution criteria properly recovered and analyzed, probability is well-calibrated. The minor double-counting between Factors 1 and 2 doesn't materially affect the estimate. Approve.
